steps.instruction: A random sample gives every member of the population an equal chance of being selected.
steps.work: Method: assign each student a number and use a random number generator to select 30.
steps.explanation: Random samples are likely to be representative of the whole population.

steps.instruction: Surveying only students at lunch table 1 is biased — those students may not represent the whole school.
steps.work: Biased sample → conclusions may not apply to the whole population.
steps.explanation: Convenience samples and voluntary response samples tend to be biased.

steps.instruction: Inferences from biased samples can be misleading.
steps.work: If you ask only athletes about exercise, you'll overestimate how much the average student exercises.
steps.explanation: Always consider whether the sample is truly representative.

steps.instruction: In a random sample of 80 students, 20 prefer reading as a hobby.
steps.explanation: This proportion is our best estimate for the whole population.

steps.instruction: The school has 640 students. Predict how many prefer reading.
steps.explanation: Apply the sample proportion to the full population.

steps.instruction: This is an estimate, not an exact count.
steps.work: We predict about 160 students prefer reading, but the actual number may differ somewhat.
steps.explanation: Larger samples give more reliable estimates.
